subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 14. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your job is to talk, sort, and draw alongside the child using the simple scripts and activities set out here. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Sort a set of objects into groups using one rule, such as colour, size, shape or type.
Understand that the sorting rule (criteria) decides which group each thing goes in.
Sort things into and out of a circle (a simple Venn diagram) — ‘belongs’ or ‘does not belong’.
Sort things into a four-box Carroll diagram using a yes/no rule, with each thing in exactly one box.
Read a simple pictogram where one picture means one thing.
Make a simple pictogram or block graph by drawing one picture or block for each object.
Count the pictures in each row or column to find how many are in each group.
Compare groups using the words more, fewer/less, most and least/fewest.
Answer simple questions about a chart, such as ‘Which has the most?’ and ‘How many more?’.

concepts.title: Sorting by One Attribute
concepts.explanation: Everything in this unit begins with sorting: putting things that are alike into the same group. The most important idea is that we sort using a rule — colour, size, shape, or type — and the rule decides where each thing belongs. Young children sort naturally when they tidy toys, so start there and simply give the activity its name. How to teach it: Tip out a basket of stones, dates and buttons onto the mat. Say, ‘Let us sort these. Our rule is colour.’ Make a small pile for each colour and place a few objects…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Look at all these stones. Let us put them into groups. Our rule is colour.” You: “Where does this white stone go?” Child: “With the white ones!” You: “Well done. Now count this group with me.…

concepts.title: The Sorting Circle (Venn Diagram)
concepts.explanation: A Venn diagram is just a circle with a rule. Things that fit the rule go inside the circle; things that do not stay outside. For Year 1 we use only one circle — overlapping circles come much later. Make the circle real: a loop of string or a scarf on the floor is far better than a drawing at this age. How to teach it: Lay a string loop on the mat and say, ‘Inside this circle we put red things. Everything else stays outside.’ Hand the child objects one at a time — a red button, a green date, a red bead — and ask,…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Inside the circle goes everything that is red. Ready?” You: “Here is a red button. Inside or outside?” Child: “Inside!” You: “Here is a green leaf. Inside or outside?” Child: “Outside — it is…

concepts.title: The Sorting Box (Carroll Diagram)
concepts.explanation: A Carroll diagram is a box divided into sections that sorts by a yes/no rule. The simplest version has just two boxes — for example ‘furry’ and ‘not furry’ — and every object must go in exactly one box. This is a gentle first step and is plenty for many Year 1 children; a full Carroll diagram with four boxes uses two rules at once and can be offered to confident children as an extension. How to teach it: Fold a sheet of paper in two and label the halves ‘furry’ and ‘not furry’ (draw a little picture as a reminder,…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “This side is for furry animals. This side is for not-furry animals.” You: “Is a camel furry?” Child: “Yes!” You: “So which box?” Child: “The furry box.” You: “Is a fish furry?” Child: “No.”…

concepts.title: Reading a Pictogram
concepts.explanation: A pictogram shows data using pictures in neat rows, where one picture means one thing. Because the rows line up, the child can see at a glance which group is biggest without even counting — the longest row is the most. Start by *reading* ready-made pictograms before asking the child to make one. How to teach it: Show the ‘Favourite fruit’ pictogram idea — a row of five mangoes, a row of three bananas, a row of two oranges — and make sure it has a title so we know what it shows. Point to each row and count:…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “This chart shows favourite fruit. Each picture means one person.” You: “Let us count the mango row. One, two, three, four, five.” You: “Which row is the longest?” Child: “Mango!” You: “So…

concepts.title: Making a Pictogram or Block Graph
concepts.explanation: A block graph is the pictogram's simple cousin: instead of a picture, we colour one block for each thing. Blocks are quicker to draw than pictures, so block graphs are a lovely way for a small child to make their own chart. The rule is the same — one block per object, no gaps, all starting from the same line. How to teach it: Run a tiny survey first. Ask three or four family members, ‘What is your favourite animal — goat, camel or chicken?’ Record each answer with a tally mark or a sticky note. Then build the…
concepts.example: Keep the child's very first charts small — three groups of a few objects each is perfect. The point is not a beautiful chart but the understanding that each block or picture stands for one real thing, and that a taller…

concepts.title: Comparing Groups — More, Fewer, Most, Least
concepts.explanation: The reason we sort and chart at all is to compare. This concept threads through every other one. Give the child the four comparing words and use them constantly: more, fewer (or less), most, least (or fewest). ‘Most’ is the biggest group of all; ‘least’ is the smallest. How to teach it: Put two groups side by side — say 6 dates and 4 dates — and ask, ‘Which has more? Which has fewer?’ The most reliable check for a young child is matching one-to-one: pair a date from each group; whichever group has dates left over…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Here are the dogs and the fish from our chart. Which row is longer?” Child: “The dogs.” You: “So there are more dogs. Dogs are the most.” You: “How many more dogs than fish? Let us match them…

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

rules.title: Sort a set of objects into groups
rules.text: Sort a set of objects into groups using one rule, such as colour, size, shape or type.

rules.title: Understand that the sorting rule (criteria) decides
rules.text: Understand that the sorting rule (criteria) decides which group each thing goes in.

rules.title: Sort things into a four-box Carroll diagram
rules.text: Sort things into a four-box Carroll diagram using a yes/no rule, with each thing in exactly one box.

rules.title: Objects that vary in an obvious way
rules.text: Objects that vary in an obvious way — different colours, sizes or shapes — so a sorting rule is easy to see.

rules.title: Everything in this unit begins with sorting
rules.text: Everything in this unit begins with sorting: putting things that are alike into the same group. The most important idea is that we sort using a rule — colour, size, shape, or type — and the rule decides where each thing belongs. Young children sort naturally when they tidy toys, so start there and simply give the activity its name.

rules.title: How to teach it: Tip out a
rules.text: How to teach it: Tip out a basket of stones, dates and buttons onto the mat. Say, ‘Let us sort these. Our rule is colour.’ Make a small pile for each colour and place a few objects together, then let the child continue. When the piles are done, count each one aloud: ‘Three brown, five white, two black.’ Then do the same objects again with a new rule — ‘Now let us sort by size: big things here, small things there.’ Seeing the *same* objects fall into *different* groups when the rule changes is the heart of sorting.

terms: The idea we use to sort
A set of things that are alike in one way
A chart that uses pictures to show data
A chart that uses one block for each thing
The name that tells what a chart shows

questions.question: Here is some data: 2, 6, 13, 11, 15, 2. Which value appears most often (the mode)?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

questions.explanation: 9 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

rounds.prompt: A tally shows these counts: 7, 3, 15, 11, 16. What is the total?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

rounds.prompt: Here is some data: 9, 13, 2, 18, 1. What is the largest value?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

rounds.prompt: Here is some data: 13, 9, 12, 15, 6. What is the range?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

rounds.prompt: What does “Rule / criteria” mean?
choices: The idea we use to sort
A set of things that are alike in one way
A chart that uses pictures to show data
A chart that uses one block for each thing
rounds.answer: The idea we use to sort
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 14 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Rule / criteria means The idea we use to sort.

games.title: Rule Runner: Group

rounds.prompt: Here is some data: 2, 6, 13, 11, 15, 2. Which value appears most often (the mode)?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
rounds.explanation: 2 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

games.title: Unit Champion: Rule / criteria

rounds.prompt: A tally shows these counts: 7, 3, 15, 11, 16. What is the total?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

rounds.prompt: Here is some data: 9, 13, 2, 18, 1. What is the largest value?

selfAssessment: I can sort a set of objects into groups using one rule, such as colour, size, shape or type.
I can understand that the sorting rule (criteria) decides which group each thing goes in.
I can sort things into and out of a circle (a simple Venn diagram) — ‘belongs’ or ‘does not belong’.
I can sort things into a four-box Carroll diagram using a yes/no rule, with each thing in exactly one box.
I can read a simple pictogram where one picture means one thing.
I can make a simple pictogram or block graph by drawing one picture or block for each object.
I can count the pictures in each row or column to find how many are in each group.
I can compare groups using the words more, fewer/less, most and least/fewest.
